data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1300 ET - Pure Storage's strong 2Q report reflects widespread growth across categories, but it's tough to find an exact catalyst for the results, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note. "Our only conundrum is we can't point to any exact factors that are catalyzing PSTG's outperformance now specifically," the analysts say, noting competitor NetApp's relatively disappointing results suggest any pickup in storage sales likely isn't broad based. Nevertheless, with better trends across the board, the analysts raise their price target on Pure Storage's stock to $80 from $70. Shares rise 31% to $79.62. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1235 ET - Pure Storage continued to make progress on its engagement with Meta and other hyperscaler customers in its latest quarter, setting the stage for a key long-term growth driver, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. Pure Storage management noted Meta initiated its first volume deployments, resulting in the first revenue contributions from the collaboration, and the company is looking to enable its other hyperscaler customers to replace raw storage with its DirectFlash Modules. The analysts say revenue contributions from such engagements will be minimal this fiscal year, but will be a key component of expectations for further accelerating growth in the future. They raise their price target on the stock to $80 from $70. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1205 ET - Pure Storage is now on track to accelerate growth this year and beyond after seeing significant revenue momentum in its latest quarter, helped by broad-based strength across its portfolio, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The company's raised guidance reflects its growing confidence in customers' spending intentions as the underlying macroeconomic environment remains more resilient than feared earlier in the year, the analysts say. Pure Storage's revenue performance and boosted outlook stand out relative to peers with more modest upsides and consistent outlooks, they say, noting such competitors are more exposed to a pullback in public sector spending. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)Delivery Hero reported first-half results. "Delivery Hero's Adjusted Earnings Guidance Cut Likely to Disappoint Investors — Market Talk," at 1043 GMT, misstated the company name.

0728 ET - Delivery Hero's results show solid growth and improving profitability, Mwb Research's Alexander Zienkowicz writes in a note. Regional trends have improved since the first quarter, with Asia rebounding as Foodpanda returned to growth and Woowa's in-house delivery expanded. Meanwhile, the Americas sustained double-digit top-line growth and Ebitda turned positive, he says. Operational momentum remains strong, cash flow and the company's balance sheet have strengthened, he adds. The only setback was foreign exchange rate headwinds, which led to a cut in full-year adjusted Ebitda guidance. Shares are down 1.9% at 23 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0642 ET - Delivery Hero reported solid first-half results but the cut to 2025 adjusted Ebitda guidance is likely to weigh on shares, Citi analysts write in a note. The downgrade sets adjusted Ebitda at between 900 million euros and 940 million euros, down from the previously guided range of 975 million euros and 1.025 billion euros. This implies a 16% shortfall versus consensus, they say. Shares are down 1.9% at 22.99 euros.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com) Corrections & Amplifications

This article was corrected at 08:03 a.m. ET to fix the spelling of the company name.

0516 ET - Delivery Hero's cut to its 2025 adjusted Ebitda guidance raises questions, UBS analysts write in a note. The German food-delivery company now expects adjusted Ebitda to be in the range of 900 million euros and 940 million euros, down from 975 million euros to 1.025 billion euros, citing foreign exchange rate headwinds. This exchange rate pressure was acknowledged earlier in the year, so the reason driving the downgrade is unclear, the analysts add. The group's solid first-half results add to this uncertainty, they say. Shares are down 0.8% at 23.25 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0509 ET - Nvidia's outlook for the current quarter is underwhelming since it doesn't include sales of H20 artificial-intelligence chips to China, Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB, writes in a note. The U.S. restricted sales of H20 chips to China in April, costing Nvidia billions of dollars. However, Nvidia received assurances from the Trump administration last month that it could sell them again. For the current quarter, Nvidia expects overall sales of $54 billion, plus or minus 2%, assuming no shipments of H20 chips to China. "Without clear guidance on China revenues, it is hard to see what the next driver of Nvidia's share price will be," Brooks says. Nvidia shares are down 1.6% premarket at $178.77. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0450 ET - The fall in Nvidia's stock price Wednesday aftermarket and Thursday premarket indicates that investors are disappointed on the China front. The tech giant didn't sell any H20 artificial-intelligence chips to China-based customers in the fiscal second quarter ended July 27 even though the Trump administration lifted export restrictions last month. "The longer it takes to generate sales in China, the more likely it is that Chinese consumers will turn to domestic chip makers who could eventually rival Nvidia's chips for their advanced capabilities," Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB, writes in a note to clients. Nvidia shares are down 1.5% premarket at $178.83. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0449 ET - PPF's decision to sell its stake in German broadcaster ProSiebenSat.1 Media supports consolidation efforts, Equita analyst Milo Silvestre writes in a note. The Czech investment conglomerate is tendering around 15.7% of ProSiebenSat.1's share capital into Netherlands-based media broadcasting group MFE-MediaForEurope's $2.4 billion takeover offer. PPF's participation in the takeover bid is supportive for MFE's stock, giving MFE control of ProSiebenSat.1 and removing uncertainty over the asset's future, he says. The consolidation is also strategically sound, strengthening MFE's presence in one of Europe's largest free-to-air advertising markets, he adds. MFE and ProSiebenSat.1 shares rise 6.4% and 2.1%, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0435 ET - Nvidia shares edge lower Thursday premarket after sales at the tech giant's data-center business and guidance for the current quarter missed analysts' forecasts. Nvidia stock closed 0.1% lower at $181.60 Wednesday ahead of results. Shares are down 1.9% at $178.21 Thursday premarket. Sales from Nvidia's data-center business climbed 56% to $41.1 billion in the fiscal second quarter ended July 27, slightly below a FactSet estimate of $41.30 billion. For the current quarter, Nvidia expects overall sales of $54 billion, plus or minus 2%, assuming no shipments of H20 chips to China. Analysts expect sales of $54.06 billion for the quarter, according to FactSet. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)Chinese Shares reversed earlier losses to end higher. "Chinese Shares End Higher, Supported by Chip Stocks — Market Talk," at 0757 GMT, misstated that Chinese shares reversed earlier gains.

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1421 ET - Cooper Cos.'s solid margins and improving free cash flow should help the company's stock despite the company's poor third-quarter earnings, say Stifel analysts, who label the stock as a "buy." The company's adjusted operating margin of 26.1% beat Wall Street estimates and Cooper said operating efficiencies are expected to continue through a restructuring in 2026. Free cash flow also beat expectations by a wide margin with working-capital efficiencies expected to continue bolstering the company's cash in coming years. The stock is down 11%, to $66.27, Thursday. That decline gives investors an opportunity to buy, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

2058 ET - Private hospital operator Ramsay Health Care drops 13% to A$33.18, with RBC Capital Markets pointing to a hat-trick of negative news. Ramsay's Australian and U.K. earnings in FY 2025 were weaker than expected. Ramsay also pointed to a A$37 million hit to EBIT coming in FY 2026 from a new funding agreement that affects its Joondalup campus in Western Australia. Also included in its guidance are projected net financing costs of A$600 million-A$620 million, which RBC says are higher than consensus expectations. Ramsay's share price fall on Thursday virtually erases all gains since hitting a low in mid-April. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2044 ET - Pharmacy group Ebos's disappointing FY 2025 result costs it a bull. Forsyth Barr downgrades Ebos to neutral from outperform, and cuts its price target by 19% to NZ$37.25 after making material reductions to its earnings forecasts and projecting higher capex. Analyst Matt Montgomerie says the drivers of Ebos's softer-than-expected result and outlook commentary don't appear to be structural. Forsyth Barr still sees Ebos as positioned for medium-term growth. "But at 23x our revised 12-month forward EPS with a high single-digit three-year forward EPS compound annual growth rate, we don't see valuation upside to retain outperform," Forsyth Barr says. Ebos is down 5.5% at NZ$32.00. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1249 ET - Industry brand loyalty rates declined slightly in the first half of 2025 following last year's rebound, according to a S&P Global Mobility analysis of new vehicle registration data June. The industry's brand loyalty rate through June stood at 51.1%, down 1.4 percentage points from the same period in 2024. The decrease marks a reversal from 2024's year-over-year gain, showing continued volatility in loyalty trends as the market continues to stabilize post-pandemic. Both mainstream and luxury brands contributed to the decline. The number of households returning to market rose 4.2% year-over-year, representing the third consecutive year of improvement. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1005 ET - Falling mortgage rates have pushed the median U.S. monthly mortgage payment down to $2,616, its lowest level since the start of the year, according to Redfin. The weekly average mortgage rate is sitting at 6.58%, its lowest level in 10 months. Lower housing costs are bringing some house hunters off the sidelines. Pending home sales rose 1.6% year over year during the four weeks ending Wednesday. That marks two straight months of increases in pending sales after they fell for most of 2025. But many buyers are still waiting on the sidelines. A lot of prospective buyers are waiting to see if the Fed cuts interest rates in September to buy. But Redfin economists say today's mortgage rates likely already reflect the upcoming rate cut. On the selling side, new listings are up 1.9% year over year. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0740 ET - Switzerland's midcap insurers first-half results aren't set to be particularly exciting, though the subsector could still benefit from strong currency trends, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a note. Helvetia and Baloise--which recently agreed to merge--and Swiss Life will publish their results in September. Swiss primary insurers have had good performances since the start of the year, and their shares have outperformed the Stoxx 600 insurance sub-index, analyst Michele Ballatore writes. Swiss Life's results could see some weakness based on a strong comparative, while the focus for Helvetia and Baloise will be on their upcoming integration, he adds. "Operationally, it should be a relatively benign quarter in [property and casualty] with some headwinds from FX," Ballatore says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0500 ET - Santander's return on tangible equity could reach 20%, Morgan Stanley says in a research note. The Spanish bank could unveil a more than 18% ROTE target at its February investor day, analyst Alvaro Serrano writes. Thanks to the integration of TSB in the U.K., the deployment of its Gravity digital banking platform, and the normalization of its Brazil earnings as the U.S. Federal Reserve has opened the door to lower interest rates, the analyst sees a path to a ROTE of 18% to 19%. This could increase by a further percentage point if the profitability of the bank's U.S. retail operations is addressed, he adds. Shares have risen 84% since the start of the year. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0449 ET - PPF's decision to sell its stake in German broadcaster ProSiebenSat.1 Media supports consolidation efforts, Equita analyst Milo Silvestre writes in a note. The Czech investment conglomerate is tendering around 15.7% of ProSiebenSat.1's share capital into Netherlands-based media broadcasting group MFE-MediaForEurope's $2.4 billion takeover offer. PPF's participation in the takeover bid is supportive for MFE's stock, giving MFE control of ProSiebenSat.1 and removing uncertainty over the asset's future, he says. The consolidation is also strategically sound, strengthening MFE's presence in one of Europe's largest free-to-air advertising markets, he adds. MFE and ProSiebenSat.1 shares rise 6.4% and 2.1%, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0437 ET - Bitcoin exchange-traded funds are seeing improved demand after recent capital outflows, Deus X Pay director James Madden says. Spot bitcoin ETFs from Fidelity and BlackRock have broken their outflow streak, bringing in $219 million in daily inflows, he says. "This shift reflects a renewed interest in bitcoin, demonstrating that these funds are gaining traction after a challenging period," Madden says. The influx of capital into these ETFs signals confidence in bitcoin's long-term potential and reinforces the narrative of institutional adoption of cryptocurrencies, he says. Bitcoin rises 0.7% to $113,225, recovering after hitting a nearly seven-week low of $108,829 Tuesday, LSEG data show. However, it remains well below the record high of $124,480 reached on August 14.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

2337 ET - Hong Leong Bank's earnings momentum is expected to continue, supported by solid fundamentals and a reduced reliance associate contributions, according to RHB IB analysts Nabil Thoo and David Chong. They note management's net interest margin guidance of 1.8%-1.9% for FY 2026 after factoring in the policy rate cut in July and tighter liquidity. Management also sees opportunities to monetize its fixed-income securities to cushion the earnings downside, they say in a note. The lender is optimistic about the outlook for China associate Bank of Chengdu while remaining open to potential value-unlocking opportunities, they add. RHB raises the target price to MYR25.70 from MYR24.30 with a buy rating on the stock, a top sector pick. Shares rise 3.2% to MYR20.38. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1523 ET - Oil futures settle higher after spending much of the session in the red, with the market watching increased strikes in the Russia-Ukraine war just weeks after hopes were raised for peace talks, while prospects for looser oil balances after the high-demand summer season keep buyers cautious. "Traders are glued to that narrow range," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. "It's the classic push and pull-bulls and bears in a stalemate while everyone waits for the next shoe to drop." WTI settles up 0.7% at $64.60 a barrel. Brent rises 0.8% to $67.62 ahead of tomorrow's October contract expiration. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0844 ET - Oil futures are slightly lower with the market focused on Russia-Ukraine and expectations for looser market balances after the summer. Russia-related tariffs and sanctions, along with attacks on Russian oil facilities are keeping crude prices resilient "despite the looming oversupply that should lower prices, particularly after the Mideast summer burn for cooling purposes and the end of the summer driving season," Anthony Yuen of Citi Research says in a note. He attributes that to OPEC+ output increases being less than expected, while "the amount of Indian diversification away from Russian oil purchases could also be greater than reported, as reflected in a much tighter Brent-Dubai spread." Citi keeps its average 3Q price forecast for Brent at $66 a barrel, and sees the benchmark averaging $63 in 4Q as "oversupply should start to exert greater impact on the oil supply-demand balance into year end." Brent is off 0.3% at $67.87 and WTI is off 0.2% at $64.04.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0635 ET - Drax's agreement with the U.K. government to keep its North Yorkshire power plant open after 2027 through the use of subsidies could face additional political scrutiny, Jefferies analysts write. This is after the Financial Conduct Authority launched an investigation into certain historical statements regarding its biomass sourcing and the compliance of some annual reports. The investigation appears to be at a very early stage, which means limited information is currently available, the analysts write. Shares trade down 9% at 630.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0539 ET - Orsted's shares will remain under pressure over the near term as it battles uncertainty in the U.S. over its Revolution Wind project and undertakes a rights issue, Berenberg analysts write. However, under all scenarios, including the unlikely situation that it can't divest assets and is forced to cancel its U.S. offshore projects, the Danish energy company will be able to cover capital expenditure through 2027, they write. This is because of its planned $9.4 billion rights issue, they add. A share price of 172 Danish krone is seen as a floor in its valuation, they write. The analysts upgrade the stock's rating to buy, from hold, but with a reduced target price of 225 krone, from 250 krone. Shares trade up 1.9% at 192.65 krone. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0448 ET - Shares in power producer Drax trade lower after the U.K.'s financial watchdog started an investigation into the company's biomass sourcing and recent annual reports. The probe could increase uncertainty over an agreement with the U.K. government to extend the company's power station, RBC Capital Markets analysts say. There is no indication on why it was launched or when it will conclude, the analysts say. Shares trade down 9% at 640 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0316 ET - Cnooc's shares could rise given its breakthroughs in oil and gas exploration and discoveries, Citi's Desmond Law and Oscar Yee say in a note. The Chinese energy company made five new offshore discoveries in China and appraised several large- and medium-sized oil and gas bearing structures in 1H. It looks poised to meet its 2025 target reserve replacement ratio of not less than 130%, they say. Cnooc also advanced its deep water exploration campaign in Guyana and signed its first oil contract for exploration in a new Kazakhstan block, which enlarges its overseas exploration portfolio. Citi maintains its buy rating and HK$24.00 target. Cnooc's Hong Kong-listed shares gain 4.1% to HK$19.41. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0135 ET - QL Resources' earnings are expected to recover in 2H FY2026, supported by its palm oil and clean energy segment via robust solar demand, CIMB Securities analyst Walter Aw Lik Hsin says in a note. A rebound in the marine-product manufacturing segment given a low FY2025 base on softer surimi prices and firmer export demand, as well as improved convenience-store performance through marketing initiatives, are also likely to support the earnings outlook, he says. For its integrated livestock farming segment, the removal of government subsidies for chicken eggs may weigh on profits, but higher egg supply, price adjustments, and margin gains from a stronger ringgit should partly offset the impact, he adds. CIMB cuts QL's target price to MYR4.50 from MYR4.85, while maintaining a hold rating on the stock. Shares are 0.2% higher at MYR4.16. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2102 ET - Karoon Energy could unlock US$200 million-US$300 million of value from the Neon oil field offshore Brazil if it sells a 40% interest, says Citi. Karoon plans to open a data room to potential investors in Neon next month. A deal could reduce the capex risk from developing a deposit that won't come online until 2029, analyst Tom Wallington says. It would also add balance-sheet flexibility from 2H 2026. Citi includes the Neon sell-down among a range of catalysts that support its buy call on the stock. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2040 ET - Meridian Energy's FY 2025 result was terrible but it's highly unlikely to repeat this in the new fiscal year, Forsyth Barr says. More so if Meridian is successful in obtaining access to contingent hydro storage that currently can only be accessed in extreme situations, analyst Andrew Harvey-Green says. Forsyth Barr upgrades Meridian to outperform, from neutral. Meridian held its dividend flat at NZ$0.21/share in FY 2025, but noted the impact this had on its balance sheet. "That said, the risk of a dividend cut is low," Forsyth Barr says. Meridian's net debt-to-Ebitdaf ratio is likely to be 1.5X at the end of FY 2026. "Under normal circumstances Meridian has plenty of balance-sheet capacity," Forsyth Barr says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Dell Beat Earnings Estimates. Why the Stock Is Down. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Shares of Dell Technologies were trading lower late Thursday after the computer maker reported better-than-expected second-quarter financials but a third-quarter outlook that missed the mark.

Dell reported second-quarter adjusted earnings of $2.32 on revenue of $29.78 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet were expecting the PC and server firm to report earnings of $2.29 a share on revenue of $29 billion.

Dell reports revenue for both its Client Solutions Group, which includes PCs and other commercial hardware, and its Infrastructure Solutions Group, which includes servers that help power artificial intelligence.

Client Solutions Group revenue of $12.5 billion missed Wall Street estimates for $12.97 billion, while Infrastructure Solutions Group Revenue of $16.8 billion beat expectations of $15.64 billion.

"Demand for our AI solutions continues to be exceptional, and we're raising our AI server shipment guidance," Jeff Clarke, chief operating officer, said in the earnings release.

Dell now expects AI server shipment guidance for fiscal 2026 to be $20 billion, up from prior guidance of $15 billion.

However, Dell also said it expects third-quarter adjusted earnings to be $2.45 a share at the midpoint, compared to analyst estimates of $2.55 a share.

Shares of Dell were down 3.8% in after hours trading following the results.

This is breaking news. Please come back for more updates and analysis and read below for a preview of Dell's earnings.

HP Inc.'s stronger-than-expected demand for personal computers could be a good omen for Dell Technologies, which reports earnings after the stock market closes Thursday.

HP reported financial results for its fiscal third-quarter after the market close on Wednesday. Earnings for the quarter were in-line with analyst estimates, while revenue came in above expectations.

"Despite fears of a PC demand slow-down owing to a tariff related pull-in last quarter, HP reported solid [revenue]," UBS analyst David Vogt wrote in a note. He raised his price target on HP to $29 from $26 and maintained a Neutral rating on the stock.

Shares of HP rose 2.8% to $27.87 on Thursday.

President Donald Trump has implemented tariffs in what he says is a bid to bring manufacturing back to the U.S. However, some tech hardware is exempt from the highest tariffs as Section 232 investigations into electronic products continue. Those investigations allow the U.S. government to look into whether imports of certain products are a threat to national security.

Even though HP PCs aren't affected by tariffs, there's a concern that consumers' purchasing power will suffer from the general increase of goods prices from higher import taxes. But so far, that hasn't happened yet.

HP CEO Enrique Lores told Barron's on Wednesday that HP believes the back-to-school season is "starting strong," which helped demand.

Also, Microsoft's Windows 10 operating system ends in October. When that happens, technical assistance and software updates will no longer be provided on Windows 10 systems, unless customers pay per device. Not only has this helped incentivize purchases of new PCs, but HP says that only about 50% of the installed base has been converted to Windows 11, leaving more room for upgrades in the months ahead.

This could all be a good sign for PC maker Dell.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect Dell to report second-quarter adjusted earnings of $2.29 a share on revenue of $29 billion.

Dell reports revenue for both its Client Solutions Group, which includes PCs and other commercial hardware, and its Infrastructure Solutions Group, which includes servers that help power artificial intelligence.

Analysts expect Dell to report revenue of $12.87 billion for its Client Solutions Group, which would be a 3.7% increase from the same period last year.

Wall Street will also be paying close attention to Dell's server demand for the quarter, as artificial-intelligence infrastructure investments remain a key focus for investors. Analysts expect Infrastructure Solutions Group revenue of $15.64 billion, a 34% jump from last year.

"Our supply chain checks suggest that AI server order momentum remains strong," BofA Securities analyst Wamsi Mohan wrote in a note on Aug. 15. He forecasts AI server revenue of $20.7 billion in the fiscal year 2026, above Dell's current guidance of more than $15 billion, he added.

He rates Dell as a Buy with a $165 price target.

Shares of Dell were up 0.5% to $133.12 Thursday. The stock has gained 16% this year.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0838 ET - Sibanye Stillwater delivered a relatively strong set of first-half results with Ebitda and free cash flow beating consensus expectations, RBC Capital Markets analysts write. A positive element was management appearing to open the door to delaying the commissioning and production at its Keliber plant given the current lithium market conditions, they say. This suggests that the South African precious-metals miner is willing to restructure low-margin operations, they say. The company's exposure to gold and platinum group metals, combined with the potential for a portfolio restructure under its new management, should drive a re-rating of its shares. Shares trade down 2.2% at 3,402 rand ($192). (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0500 ET - Hochschild Mining is unlikely to get full operations up and running at its Mara Rosa mine in Brazil until the second half 2026, Berenberg analysts write. Despite previously flagging woes at the open-pit gold mine, Wednesday's production guidance cut and raised cost expectations were more severe than expected and missed consensus expectations, they add. Investors will likely welcome an updated long-term plan for the mine in 2026 once the operational outlook is clearer, the analysts write. Operational challenges aren't the only headwind the miner faces, with inflation in Argentina pushing costs higher, they write. Shares trade down 2.2% at 271.80 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2245 ET - Palm oil futures fall in Asia trade, tracking weaker soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. Rising palm oil output is also weighing on market sentiment in the crude palm oil market, he says. The Malaysian Palm Oil Association reported a 3.0% on-month increase in CPO production for Aug. 1-20. Ng expects support for CPO futures at 4,400 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,580 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for October delivery is down MYR2 at MYR4,486 a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2230 ET - Rapidly improved rare-earths market conditions have allowed Lynas to tap equity markets to support growth plans to 2030, Jefferies analyst Mitch Ryan says in a note. "Securing strategic new offtakes or downstream partnerships would support [its] current valuation," says Ryan, who adds that Lynas is well-positioned to achieve that. He says Lynas's FY 2025 profit results are stronger than expectations. Jefferies is positive on its medium- to long-term outlook, with Lynas strategically placed to take advantage of strength in the rare-earths market, Ryan says. Jefferies has a buy rating and a A$12.50 target on Lynas. Shares are halted and last traded at A$14.73. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2027 ET - South32's portfolio continues to pivot toward base metals, "with Hermosa remaining the core long-term valuation anchor," Jefferies analyst Mitch Ryan says in a note following the miner's FY profit result. South32 missed profit expectations, and its dividend was also a tad light, Ryan notes. FY 2026 guidance also points to higher operating costs across most operations, he says. Jefferies has a hold rating and A$3.10 target on South32. The stock is down 3.1% at A$2.82. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2020 ET - South32's FY 2025 underlying profit is 5% below consensus and its FY 2026 cost forecasts are higher than envisaged, a modest negative, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart. "Unit cost guidance for FY26 shows modest cost inflation," he says. Underlying Ebitda and net cash are in line with expectations, McTaggart adds. Citi has a neutral rating and A$3.20 target on South32. The stock is down 2.1% at A$2.85. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1249 ET - Industry brand loyalty rates declined slightly in the first half of 2025 following last year's rebound, according to a S&P Global Mobility analysis of new vehicle registration data June. The industry's brand loyalty rate through June stood at 51.1%, down 1.4 percentage points from the same period in 2024. The decrease marks a reversal from 2024's year-over-year gain, showing continued volatility in loyalty trends as the market continues to stabilize post-pandemic. Both mainstream and luxury brands contributed to the decline. The number of households returning to market rose 4.2% year-over-year, representing the third consecutive year of improvement. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0408 ET - HD Hyundai Heavy Industries could see a surge in backlog orders, revenue and operating profit after a planned merger with smaller shipbuilder HD Hyundai Mipo, Nomura analysts Eon Hwang and Heesoo Min write in a research note. The two South Korean shipmakers plan to merge into one entity through a share-for-share exchange by December, with HD Hyundai Heavy Industries set to become the continuing entity. The analysts expect HD Hyundai Heavy's post-merger backlog orders to jump 31% to $51 billion. They also expect HD Hyundai Heavy's revenue to climb 30% to KRW22 trillion and operating profit to increase 20% to KRW2.3 trillion after the merger. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2035 ET - Jefferies analysts and Qantas bulls Anthony Moulder and Amit Kanwatia say the outlook for Australia's largest carrier is looking positive. The Jefferies analysts say the airline's FY 2025 underlying profit before tax result was slightly ahead of market expectations, and that travel demand is expected to remain strong through 1H FY 2026. That will support higher capacity growth than expected, they note. Qantas said earlier today that its FY 2025 underlying profit before tax rose some 15%. Investors seem pleased, with Qantas shares rising 13% in recent trade. (mike.cherney@wsj.com)

data.title: Avnet Files 8K - Entry Into Definitive Agreement >AVT
data.description:
Avnet Inc. (AVT) filed a Form 8K - Entry Into a Definitive Agreement - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on August 28, 2025.

On August 28, 2025, Avnet, Inc. (the "Company") and Avnet Holding Europe BV ("Avnet Europe") entered into (x) Amendment No. 1 to the Third Amended and Restated Credit Agreement, dated January 17, 2025 (the "Third Amended Credit Agreement"), among the Company, Avnet Europe, Bank of America, N.A., as the administrative agent, and the lenders party thereto (the "RCF Credit Amendment") and (y) Amendment No. 1 to the Credit Agreement, dated July 1, 2025 (the "Term Loan Credit Agreement" and together with the Third Amended Credit Agreement, the "Credit Agreements"), among Avnet Europe, Bank of America, N.A., as the administrative agent, and the lenders party thereto (the "TL Credit Amendment" and, together with the RCF Credit Amendment, the "Credit Amendments").

Under the terms of the Credit Amendments, among other things, the maximum consolidated leverage ratio (as defined in each Credit Agreement) was temporarily increased from 4.00 to 1.00 to (a) 5.00 to 1.00, commencing with the four fiscal quarter period ending on or around September 30, 2025 through (and including) the four fiscal quarter period ending on or around March 31, 2026; and (b) 4.50 to 1.00 for the four fiscal quarter period ending on or around June 30, 2026, before returning to 4.00 to 1.00 for the four fiscal quarter period ending on or around September 30, 2026 and thereafter. During the period from the date of the Credit Amendments until the maximum consolidated leverage ratio returns to 4.00 to 1.00 (the "Relief Period"), the Company and its subsidiaries are prohibited from making certain restricted payments under each Credit Agreement, but (i) the Company may continue to declare and pay cash dividends consistent with the ordinary course of business prior to the Credit Amendments, as long as it does not increase the amount or frequency of such cash dividends during the Relief Period and (ii) the Company may repurchase up to an aggregate of $100 million of shares of its common stock.

The description of the Credit Amendments set forth above is only a summary of their material terms and does not purport to be complete, and is qualified in its entirety by reference to the full and complete terms contained in the RCF Credit Amendment, which is filed as Exhibit 10.1 to this Form 8-K, and the TL Credit Amendment, which is filed as Exhibit 10.2 to this Form 8-K, and in each case, incorporated into this Item 1.01 by reference. The Credit Amendments are not intended to be a source of factual, business or operational information about the Company or its subsidiaries. The representations, warranties and covenants contained in the Credit Amendments were made only for purposes of such agreements and as of specific dates, were solely for the benefit of the parties to such agreements, and may be subject to limitations agreed upon by the parties, including being qualified by disclosures for the purpose of allocating contractual risk between the parties instead of establishing matters as facts; and may be subject to standards of materiality applicable to the contracting parties that differ from those applicable to investors or security holders. Accordingly, investors should not rely on the representations, warranties and covenants or any descriptions thereof as characterizations of the actual state of facts or condition of the parties.

Some or all of the parties to the Credit Amendments, or their affiliates, have in the past provided investment or commercial banking services to Avnet and its affiliates for which they received customary fees and expenses, and they may provide similar services in the future.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/8858/000000885825000043/avt-20250828x8k.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/8858/000000885825000043/0000008858-25-000043-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: Affirm Swings to Profit as Top Merchants Boost Volume
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Affirm swung to a profit in its fiscal fourth quarter, surpassing expectations, as its largest merchants helped boost gross merchandise volume.

The buy now, pay later company on Thursday posted a profit of $69.2 million, or 20 cents a share, in the quarter ended June 30, compared with a loss of $45.1 million, or 14 cents a share, a year earlier. Analysts expected a profit of 12 cents a share, according to FactSet.

Revenue rose 33% to $876.4 million. Analysts surveyed by FactSet forecast revenue of $837 million.

Gross merchandise volume rose 43% to $10.4 billion. Affirm's largest merchant partners helped drive that growth, with gross merchandise volume at its top five merchants and platform partners increasing 41%.

The number of active consumers, excluding Affirm's discontinued business Returnly, rose 24%.

Affirm expects gross merchandise volume in fiscal 2026 to be more than $46 billion. It anticipates revenue to be 8.4% of gross merchandise volume. In the fiscal first quarter, it projects revenue of $855 million to $885 million.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: LOWES COMPANIES INC SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Lowe's Companies Inc., a leading home improvement retailer, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of fiscal 2025. The report highlights the company's financial and operational performance, showcasing steady growth and strategic initiatives aimed at enhancing customer experience and operational efficiency.

Financial Highlights

Net Sales: $23,959 million, increased 1.6% compared to the second quarter of fiscal 2024, driven by a 1.1% increase in comparable sales.

Gross Margin: $8,101 million, representing 33.81% of net sales, improved by 34 basis points due to productivity initiatives and improvements in inventory shrink.

Operating Income: $3,469 million, representing 14.48% of net sales, slightly decreased by 13 basis points compared to the prior year.

Net Earnings: $2,398 million, consistent with the prior year, reflecting stable performance despite acquisition-related expenses.

Diluted Earnings Per Share: $4.27, increased from $4.17 in the prior year, reflecting improved operational efficiency and cost management.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company reported net sales of $23,959 million for the three months ended August 1, 2025, with Home Décor contributing 34.9% of sales, Hardlines 32.6%, Building Products 29.6%, and Other categories 2.9%. For the six months ended, Home Décor accounted for 35.4% of sales, Hardlines 31.7%, Building Products 30.4%, and Other categories 2.5%.

Comparable Sales Performance: Comparable sales increased by 1.1% in the second quarter of fiscal 2025, driven by a 2.9% increase in average ticket size, despite a 1.8% decline in customer transactions. For the first six months, comparable sales decreased by 0.3%, with a 2.5% increase in average ticket offset by a 2.8% decline in transactions.

Product Category Performance: Nine out of fourteen product categories experienced positive comparable store sales in the second quarter, with notable strength in Building Materials, Appliances, Lawn & Garden, and Tools, reflecting continued growth with Pro customers and online sales.

Operational Initiatives: The company continued its Perpetual Productivity Initiatives (PPI), focusing on SKU rationalizations to make inventory and space more productive, reducing seasonal markdowns, and streamlining the Freight Flow process to enhance efficiency.

New Initiatives: The launch of the Home Improvement Creator Network aims to drive brand loyalty among tech-savvy generations by partnering with top influencers to leverage social media-driven DIY inspiration.

Future Outlook: The company plans to continue leveraging its Total Home strategy to drive Pro customer growth and enhance online sales. The acquisition of Artisan Design Group is expected to expand the Pro customer offering into new distribution channels.

SEC Filing: LOWES COMPANIES INC [ LOW ] - 10-Q - Aug. 28, 2025

data.title: US national bar exam scores hit 12-year high
data.description:
U.S. law students awaiting their summer bar exam results have reason for optimism this year.

The national average score on July's 200-question Multistate Bar Exam was the highest since 2013, the National Conference of Bar Examiners said Thursday, excluding results from 2020 when the test was delayed or modified due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

The MBE is one of three components of the national bar exam required for attorney licensing and accounts for 50% of a bar taker’s score in most jurisdictions. The average score was 142.4 in July, up 0.6 points over the previous summer.

"Due to this increase in mean we expect to see a small increase in overall pass rates compared to last July,” said Bob Schwartz, the national conference’s managing director of psychometrics.

States will start to release scores and pass rates for the July exam in the coming weeks.

While the national average on the MBE has ticked up each year since 2022 for the July exam, that has not been the case for the February version of the test, which typically has a significantly higher portion of repeat bar examinees.

The February 2025 average MBE score was 130.8, which was the lowest on record since the MBE debuted in 1972. Some of that decline was due to California using its own bar exam for the first time and no longer using the MBE. The California Supreme Court ordered the state to return to the MBE in July after the February test there was marred by technical and logistical problems.

Read more:

National bar exam score hit record low in February

Bar exam results for July on the rise

data.title: Veeva Systems Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
This Website Unlocks the Joy of Traveling the World, One Radio Station at a Time — WSJ
data.description:
By Sebastian Modak

I have never been to Madagascar, but I have danced with abandon to the sounds of a Friday night in the capital city of Antananarivo. I've never attended a livestock auction in Iowa or a megachurch service in Bolivia, but I know they both inspire a similar fervor.

For these experiences, I can thank the website Radio Garden. The free site, also available as an app, streams feeds from radio stations all over the world on a page that displays a globe. As you click and drag your way across the planet, tiny green dots denote different stations. Over a single session, you might catch a traditional gamelan ensemble in Yogyakarta, Indonesia, a live samba show in Brazil, and an argument in Mongolian so heated, you'll find yourself picking a side before you realize you don't understand a word.

Radio Garden was released in 2016 as an art project commissioned by the Netherlands Institute for Sound and Vision. The site unexpectedly went viral, attracting 6 million visitors in its first week. In 2018, one of the designers, Jonathan Puckey started working on the project full-time and a year later turned it into a company. Puckey says growth has been steady since; today, the app sees 3 million monthly active users, and hosts 37,000 radio stations from around the world.

Puckey acknowledges that before Radio Garden, anyone could search for and listen to a favorite station online. "On the web, location has really stopped being important — you can be anywhere or nowhere at any time," he said. "But here, you are looking at a map and listening to what is being produced and listened to in that place, in that exact moment — it brings the location to life." Notably, the map has no borders; cities or towns aren't labeled until you click on a station.

At Radio Garden, no algorithm pushes you toward certain corners of its world, but you aren't alone. Curated lists by like-minded organizations like Radio Is a Foreign Country, an online station dedicated to the obscure, can fast-track you to an all-Gaelic station in Belfast or an Afrobeats party in Lagos. (A $2.99-a-month premium subscription gets rid of the noninvasive ads and lets you enable a sleep timer.)

You can save favorites and return to them later. The site's real joy, however, is in how it unlocks serendipity — that travel holy grail that's been ground under the heel of social-media sameness. While clicking around Tokyo, I found J1 Radio Gold, and soon became immersed in 1960s Japanese rock 'n' roll, even as I sat under fluorescent office lights in Manhattan. Another time, I happened to hit a certain time zone in the Middle East during the Muslim call to prayer and, as I hopped from city to city, marveled at the minute variations in melodies between broadcasts.

I first found Radio Garden during the darkest days of the Covid-19 pandemic, when I was at my most desperate for contact with the outside world. It helped me feel less alone. But I use it even more now, especially when I'm at home between trips, because of what else it offers. When I eavesdrop on just the right place, at the just the right moment, I recognize the same rush of novelty, sense of connection and delight of personal discovery that I am so often chasing on adventures around the world.

data.title: MMJ Marijuana Patients' Battles Against DEA: Trump Marijuana Decision Imminent
data.description:
Justice Delayed Is Justice Denied

The retirement of Matthew Strait and Thomas Prevoznik is not closure-it is the beginning of a reckoning with the DEA's culture of obstruction.

"Thomas Prevoznik may have pulled the trigger, but Matthew Strait loaded the gun," said Duane Boise, CEO of MMJ International Holdings. "Now the question is whether Administrator Cole will keep allowing Aarathi Haig to pull the strings, or whether he'll finally restore constitutional accountability. Patients' lives depend on it."

The Seven Year Regulatory Ordeal Blocking Medical Innovation

WASHINGTON, DC / ACCESS Newswire / August 28, 2025 / In 2025, while dozens of U.S. states have legalized cannabis in some form, one shocking reality remains: a pharmaceutical company attempting to develop FDA-approved cannabis-based medicines for devastating neurological conditions has spent seven years trapped in a regulatory maze run by the Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA).

MMJ BioPharma Cultivation, which holds FDA Orphan Drug designations and has submitted Investigational New Drug (IND) applications for treatments targeting Huntington's Disease and Multiple Sclerosis, has been forced to fight not science-but a broken, unconstitutional tribunal system inside the DEA. Patients who could benefit from life-changing therapies are left waiting.

DEA Leadership Shake-Up: But One Troubling Holdout

The DEA's Diversion Control Division-long accused of stonewalling cannabis research-is under upheaval. Matthew Strait and Thomas Prevoznik, two senior officials linked to years of obstruction, have retired. Yet the agency's controversial marijuana licensing program continues to operate through an administrative law judge (ALJ) system already invalidated by the Supreme Court.

And at the center of MMJ's stalled case remains DEA attorney Aarathi Haig, who continues to prosecute despite reported failure to maintain good standing with the New Jersey Bar. Her presence underscores a troubling double standard: the DEA demands absolute compliance from registrants while tolerating ethical lapses in its own ranks.

Constitutional Defects in DEA's Tribunal System

  • Article II Violations - Presidential Oversight Blocked:

    DEA ALJs are shielded by multiple layers of tenure protection, insulating them from presidential accountability. In Free Enterprise Fund v. PCAOB (2010) and Seila Law v. CFPB (2020), the Supreme Court struck down similar structures. Even the DOJ conceded in 2025 it would no longer defend these protections, calling ALJs "unaccountable and unconstitutional."

  • Seventh Amendment Violations - Jury Trial Denied:

    In SEC v. Jarkesy (2024), the Court ruled that administrative penalties require a jury trial. MMJ argues DEA's tribunals impose "life-or-death" operational penalties on companies and therefore violate the Seventh Amendment.

  • Immediate Harm - Axon v. FTC (2023):

    The Court held that being forced into an unconstitutional tribunal is itself an injury. MMJ argues it should not have to endure DEA's flawed process before seeking relief in federal court.

Regulatory Sabotage and Catch-22 Tactics

Beyond constitutional flaws, MMJ has faced a bureaucratic gauntlet of bad faith demands:

  • DEA insisted on a "bona fide supply agreement" with a DEA-registered researcher-an impossibility, since no researchers could exist without DEA first granting registrations.

  • DEA rejected MMJ's agreement with Catalent Pharma despite the company following the agency's own template.

  • Investigators nitpicked vault designs, forcing MMJ to spend $125,000 on construction upgrades to guard against far-fetched risks like underground burglars.

  • The company pays $20,000 a month in rent for a facility it cannot use.

  • NIH funding opportunities collapsed after a DOJ attorney gave MMJ false assurances about eligibility, costing the company millions in research funding.

Ethical Failures and Misconduct

The DEA's handling of MMJ has been tainted by misconduct:

  • Unlicensed Prosecutor: DEA attorney Aarathi Haig is reportedly ineligible for a Certificate of Good Standing in New Jersey, due to unpaid mandatory fees, lapsed CLE compliance, and IOLTA trust account violations. Federal law (28 U.S.C. § 530B) requires government attorneys to follow state bar rules-yet Haig remains on MMJ's case.

  • Procedural Bias: DEA's ALJ granted deadline extensions to the government but denied MMJ the same.

The Human Cost

This obstruction is not abstract. It has delayed clinical trials for patients with Huntington's and Multiple Sclerosis-two of the cruelest neurological diseases, robbing patients of mobility, cognition, and independence.

MMJ's medicines are not dispensary products-they are standardized, pharmaceutical-grade soft-gel capsules designed under FDA oversight. The DEA's obstruction denies patients hope, while wasting millions in private investment intended to advance healthcare.

A Pattern of Agency Overreach

The DEA's actions fit a broader pattern of unconstitutional abuse. In July 2025, the Third Circuit struck down the Department of Labor's in-house court system in Sun Valley Orchards v. DOL, which bankrupted a fourth-generation family farm over paperwork errors. The parallels to MMJ's ordeal are striking:

  • Petty violations inflated into crushing penalties

  • Businesses forced into unconstitutional tribunals

  • Families and patients left devastated

The Path Forward

Immediate Actions:

  • DEA Administrator Terry Cole should stay proceedings against MMJ and shift the case to federal court.

  • DEA must publish clear, workable licensing standards.

  • Congress and the DOJ Inspector General must investigate Diversion Control Division misconduct, particularly the role of Haig, Prevoznik, and Strait.

Structural Reform:

  • Abolish the DEA's unconstitutional ALJ system.

  • Transfer cannabis research oversight to the FDA.

  • Establish independent oversight to ensure transparency and accountability.

Justice Delayed Is Justice Denied

The retirement of Strait and Prevoznik is not closure-it is the beginning of a reckoning with the DEA's culture of obstruction.

"Strait may have pulled the trigger, but Prevoznik loaded the gun," said Duane Boise, CEO of MMJ International Holdings. "Now the question is whether Administrator Cole will keep allowing Aarathi Haig to pull the strings, or whether he'll finally restore constitutional accountability. Patients' lives depend on it."

The battle over MMJ's registration is about more than cannabis. It is about whether federal agencies can run shadow courts in defiance of the Constitution, whether patients will ever see FDA-grade cannabis medicines in their lifetimes, and whether the DEA serves the law-or itself.

Until resolved, one fact is undeniable: patients are paying the highest price for DEA's unconstitutional obstruction.

MMJ is represented by attorney Megan Sheehan.

CONTACT:

Madison Hisey

MHisey@mmjih.com

203-231-8583

SOURCE: MMJ International Holdings

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Our Vertiv Holdings Stock Pick Is Up 67%. Hold On for More Gains. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Jacob Sonenshine

Vertiv Holdings stock has soared since Barron's recommended buying it a year ago, and the rally isn't over.

Shares of the $49 billion company are up 67% since we published our recommendation in late August, 2024. Sales and earnings have continued to grow rapidly because the company benefits from spending on artificial intelligence.

Vertiv makes power and cooling equipment for companies in communications and manufacturing, but the majority of its revenue comes from Big Tech companies. They are spending more each year on their data centers, which enable them to create and make use of AI software.

The momentum continued in Vertiv's second-quarter results, disclosed at the end of July. Sales grew to $2.57 billion, beating the consensus call among analysts was of $2.35 billion. While profit margins dipped because of tariff-related costs, adjusted earnings per share grew 42% from a year earlier to 95 cents. Analysts had expected 83 cents.

More importantly, management raised its forecast for full-year sales by $150 million to $10 billion, suggesting growth of 24%. While some of the increase emanates from a weaker dollar, $40 million of the jump is from stronger-than-expected demand.

The forecast still looks cautious, which could set Vertiv up to exceed expectations in the remaining quarters this year. The guidance implies revenue in the third quarter will be similar to the figure for the second quarter, which would be somewhat unusual. Sales in 2024 grew 14% on a quarterly basis throughout the year.

The revenue guidance is a "modest quarter over quarter increase," J.P. Morgan analyst Stephen Tusa wrote. That "suggests it remains conservative."

It would be no surprise for Vertiv to beat expectations in the coming quarters. The company has surpassed analysts' estimates for the top and bottom line in all of the past six quarters. It has beaten EPS forecasts for the past 10 quarters.

Another positive is that margins should stabilize soon. This year's pop in tariff-related product costs shouldn't be repeated next year. Management forecast adjusted operating margins of 20% for the third quarter, compared with 18.5% in the second, as the company makes its supply chain more efficient.

All that is why analysts expect earnings to grow about 23% annually in 2026 and 2027, according to FactSet. "We regard VRT as a leading AI infrastructure player...positioned well for growth," Oppenheimer analyst Noah Kaye wrote after the earnings report.

The stock should rise if profit growth meets those expectations. While the shares trade at 30 times the EPS expected for the coming 12 months, compared with just over 22 times for the S&P 500, that is because Vertiv has so much more potential for growth.

The multiple is below its peak for this year of 42 times. It could move back toward that level if Vertiv proves analysts right and tariffs don't do any more damage to margins.

If the valuation goes that high, the stock likely would rise above its record high of $153, hit early this year, from $134 now. The 14% gain is on the small side for a relatively volatile stock that tends to make moves. Profits are growing rapidly.

Sometimes the best move to make is no move. Keep holding on to this one.

Write to Jacob Sonenshine at jacob.sonenshine@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
